flashcards.back: Bootstrapping is the process of loading the operating system kernel into memory and starting its execution when the computer is powered on. The term comes from 'pulling oneself up by one's bootstraps.' The bootstrap program (stored in firmware/ROM) is the first code executed.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between BIOS and UEFI?
flashcards.back:
BIOS: Traditional firmware, text-based, uses MBR (2 TB limit, 4 partitions), 16-bit real mode, no Secure Boot.
UEFI: Modern firmware, graphical interface, uses GPT (no practical limit, 128 partitions), 32/64-bit mode, supports Secure Boot, faster boot.

flashcards.front: What is the MBR and what does it contain?
flashcards.back: MBR (Master Boot Record) is the first 512 bytes of a disk. It contains: boot code (446 bytes) that loads the bootloader, partition table (64 bytes) with 4 partition entries, and a boot signature (2 bytes: 0x55AA).

GRUB (GRand Unified Bootloader) is the most common bootloader for Linux. It has two stages: Stage 1 (in MBR/ESP, loads Stage 2), Stage 2 (in /boot/grub, displays boot menu, loads kernel). It allows selecting which OS/kernel to boot and passes boot parameters to the kernel.

initrd (initial RAM disk) or initramfs (initial RAM filesystem) is a temporary root filesystem loaded into memory alongside the kernel during boot. It contains essential device drivers needed to access and mount the real root filesystem on disk.

The init process (PID 1) is the first user-space process started by the kernel after booting. It is the parent of all other processes. Traditional systems use SysVinit with runlevels (0-6). Modern Linux uses systemd with units and targets for parallel service startup.
